title: Erythema Multiforme
oneLiner: Acute immune-mediated mucocutaneous rash with typical target lesions; often triggered by infection (HSV) or drugs, usually self-limiting but needs careful differentiation from SJS/TEN.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Stevens-Johnson syndrome (SJS) / Toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN):** The single finding that says "this is SJS/TEN, not EM" – **widespread blistering / skin detachment >10% BSA (Nikolsky sign positive)** and **rapid progression** (hours). Do not treat as EM – escalate immediately.
- **Airway compromise in severe mucosal EM:** Look for **stridor, hoarseness, or drooling** – indicates laryngeal oedema; call anaesthetics / ICU. Rare but life-threatening if missed.
- **Sepsis from secondary infection:** If blisters/erosions become infected, **systemic inflammatory response (tachycardia, hypotension, raised lactate)** – treat as per local sepsis protocol.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Acute onset (days) of symmetrical rash, often on **extremities** (hands, feet, extensor surfaces) ± trunk
  - **Target lesions** – three zones: central dusky/purple, middle pale/vesicular, outer erythematous rim
  - **Mucosal involvement** in EM major (oral, genital, ocular) – painful erosions, haemorrhagic crusts
  - Prodrome: mild fever, malaise (especially if mycoplasma or HSV trigger)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - **Targets** – typical (concentric rings) or **atypical** (only two zones, more raised)
  - **Acantholysis** (positive Nikolsky) is **absent** in EM – if present, suspect SJS/TEN
  - **Mucosal erosions** – check lips, buccal mucosa, conjunctivae, genitalia
  - No widespread blistering or sheet-like detachment
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Urticaria** – wheals are evanescent, not fixed target lesions; no mucosal involvement
  - **Drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms (DRESS)** – fever, lymphadenopathy, eosinophilia, internal organ involvement; EM lacks eosinophilia
  - **Fixed drug eruption** – single or few round, dark plaques; recur at same site after drug re-exposure
  - **Bullous pemphigoid** – tense blisters on flexural areas; elderly; not target lesions
  - **Vasculitis (e.g. IgA vasculitis)** – palpable purpura, often on lower legs; not target lesions
  - **Pityriasis rosea** – "herald patch" followed by Christmas tree pattern; no mucosal involvement
  - **SJS/TEN** – rapid blistering, Nikolsky positive, >10% BSA detachment, severe constitutional symptoms
- **Splitter:** **Nikolsky sign** – positive in SJS/TEN, negative in EM major with mucosal involvement. Also: **skin detachment >10% BSA** is not seen in EM.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - **Nikolsky sign** – gentle lateral pressure on erythematous skin; if blister forms → SJS/TEN
  - Assess **% BSA of skin detachment** (using Wallace rule of nines)
  - Check **mucosal surfaces** (oral, ocular, genital) with good light
- **Bloods:**
  - **FBC, U&Es, LFTs, CRP** – to rule out infection, organ involvement
  - **Serology for Mycoplasma pneumoniae** (IgM + cold agglutinins) – if respiratory prodrome
  - **HSV serology / PCR** – if recurrent or genital lesions
  - **If drug-induced** – eosinophil count may be raised (but more suggestive DRESS)
- **Imaging:**
  - **Chest X-ray** – if mycoplasma suspected (interstitial pneumonitis)
- **Special tests:**
  - **Skin biopsy for histology** (if diagnostic uncertainty) – full-thickness epidermal necrosis suggests SJS/TEN; EM shows interface dermatitis with apoptotic keratinocytes
  - **Direct immunofluorescence** – not routine; may help exclude autoimmune blistering disorders
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **Bastuji-Garin criteria** – distinguishes EM from SJS/TEN: EM has **target lesions** and **no** widespread skin detachment; SJS/TEN have >30% BSA detachment (with or without targets)
  - **SCORTEN** (for SJS/TEN severity) – not validated for EM
- **Never-miss test:** **Skin biopsy for histology** if doubt between EM major and SJS/TEN – omission may lead to inappropriate treatment (e.g. steroids in SJS/TEN) or failure to escalate.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Stop any suspicious drug** – document drug history (all current and recent)
  - **Assess airway** if severe oral mucosal involvement; if stridor, call anaesthetics
  - **IV fluids** if unable to swallow / dehydrated (0.9% sodium chloride at maintenance)
  - **Analgesia** – oral paracetamol (1g QDS); for severe mucosal pain, consider IV morphine (2.5-5mg PRN) – [check local policy]
  - **If moderate/severe mucosal involvement** – admit to dermatology or medical ward
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - **Supportive care** – wound care (non-adherent dressings, chlorhexidine baths), mouth care (benzydamine spray, oral antifungal if needed)
  - **Treat underlying cause**:
    - **HSV-associated** – oral aciclovir 200mg 5x/day for 5-7 days (if herpes lesions present) – but EM often begins after HSV, so antivirals may not alter current episode. For recurrent EM, consider prophylactic aciclovir 400mg BD for 6-12 months (NICE guidance)
    - **Mycoplasma-associated** – treat with clarithromycin 500mg BD PO for 7 days (or azithromycin)
    - **Drug-induced** – stop offending drug; avoid re-exposure
  - **Systemic corticosteroids** – controversial; may be considered in severe EM major with significant mucosal involvement (e.g. prednisolone 0.5-1 mg/kg/day PO for 5-7 days) – but **do not give if SJS/TEN cannot be excluded** – [check local policy]
  - **Eye care** – if ocular involvement, urgent ophthalmology referral; topical lubricants and steroid drops if corneal ulceration
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Recurrent EM** – identify triggers (HSV, drugs); consider prophylactic aciclovir (as above)
  - **Patient education** – avoid NSAIDs, anticonvulsants, sulphonamides if history of drug-induced EM
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride at 1-2 ml/kg/hr if nil by mouth; otherwise encourage oral hydration
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** risk assess – if immobile, use enoxaparin 40mg SC daily (adjust for weight/renal function per BNF)
  - **Diet:** soft/liquid diet if oral erosions; NBM if airway risk
  - **Observations:** baseline NEWS2, then 4-hourly; escalate if NEWS ≥5 or deterioration
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Analgesia:** Paracetamol 1g PO QDS (max 4g/day). If severe: morphine sulfate 2.5-5mg IV PRN (with naloxone available)
  - **Mouth care:** Benzydamine 0.15% spray or 0.3% oral rinse 5-10ml up to 6 times/day
  - **If HSV trigger (prophylaxis for recurrent EM):** Aciclovir 400mg PO BD for 6-12 months (renally adjust – CrCl <25 ml/min: 200mg BD)
  - **If Mycoplasma trigger:** Clarithromycin 500mg PO BD for 7 days (or azithromycin 500mg PO OD for 3 days)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Corticosteroids (if severe EM major, no SJS/TEN):** Prednisolone 40-60mg PO OD for 5-7 days then taper
  - **Antihistamines** (for pruritus): Cetirizine 10mg PO OD
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Aciclovir: caution in renal impairment (reduce dose)
  - Clarithromycin: QT prolongation risk – check ECG; interacts with statins, warfarin, digoxin
  - Prednisolone: avoid in active infection; consider varicella zoster risk (check immunity)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Aciclovir: CrCl 10-25 ml/min – 200mg BD (prophylaxis); CrCl <10 – 200mg OD
  - Clarithromycin: severe hepatic impairment – reduce dose or avoid
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Prednisolone in SJS/TEN** – giving systemic steroids in early SJS/TEN may worsen outcome (increased infection/mortality). **Never start steroids until EM confirmed – if doubt, hold and discuss with dermatology.**
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - **Secondary bacterial infection** of erosions (Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus pyogenes) – treat with flucloxacillin 500mg QDS PO or IV depending on severity
  - **Ocular complications** – conjunctivitis, corneal ulceration (rare in EM minor; more in major)
  - **Dehydration from poor oral intake** – especially in children
  - **Severe oral scarring** (rare)
- **Chronic:**
  - **Recurrent EM** – can occur every few weeks/months (HSV-driven)
  - **Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation** – resolves over months; no scarring if no infection
  - **Chronic ocular sequelae** – dry eye, symblepharon (rare) – need ophthalmology follow-up
sections.Prognosis:
- **EM minor** – self-limiting in 2-4 weeks; full recovery, <5% recurrence.
- **EM major** – lasts 3-6 weeks with mucosal involvement; mortality <1% (if no progression to SJS/TEN). Recurrence rate higher if HSV trigger (up to 20%).
- **Prognosis** is excellent with supportive care; most patients do not require hospital admission.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always take a thorough drug history** including over-the-counter, herbal, and recent vaccinations – medication triggers are common.
- **Check for recurrent HSV** – ask about cold sores in the 2-4 weeks before rash. If recurrent EM, prescribe prophylactic aciclovir (even if no active HSV) after discussion with dermatology.
- **Document target lesions photographically** – helps monitor progression and differentiate from SJS/TEN. Use bedside ruler for scale.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not give systemic steroids empirically** – if mucosal involvement is severe but you cannot exclude SJS/TEN, steroids are contraindicated. Escalate to dermatology first.
- **Do not discharge without eye assessment** – even subtle conjunctival injection may indicate early ocular involvement; if missed, can lead to chronic dry eye.
- **Do not confuse atypical target lesions with urticaria** – urticarial wheals last <24 hours at a site; EM lesions are fixed for days. Antihistamines alone will not resolve EM.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - **Rash worsens or blisters enlarge** – new blistering beyond original areas or Nikolsky positive
  - **Difficulty swallowing, breathing, or stridor** – indicates laryngeal oedema (call 999)
  - **Signs of infection** – fever, oozing/redness around eroded areas, spreading erythema
  - **Eye pain, photophobia, or red eye** – indicates corneal involvement (urgent ophthalmology)
- **Follow-up:** GP review in 2 weeks; if recurrent, refer to dermatology. If ocular involvement, ophthalmology follow-up in 1 week.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / dermatology if:**
  - Uncertainty between EM major and SJS/TEN (worst-case scenario)
  - Severe mucosal involvement (oral/ocular/genital) – requiring IV fluids, ophthalmology
  - Rapid progression of blisters/new target lesions over >30% BSA in <48 hours
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**
  - **NEWS2 ≥7** or any single parameter meets criteria (respiratory rate >30, hypotension SBP <90, GCS <13)
  - **Airway compromise** (stridor, drooling, hoarseness)
  - **Lactate >4 mmol/L** or **oliguria <0.5 ml/kg/hr** for >2 hours (signs of sepsis from skin infection)

rawMarkdown:
## Erythema Multiforme
**One-liner:** Acute immune-mediated mucocutaneous rash with typical target lesions; often triggered by infection (HSV) or drugs, usually self-limiting but needs careful differentiation from SJS/TEN.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Stevens-Johnson syndrome (SJS) / Toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN):** The single finding that says "this is SJS/TEN, not EM" – **widespread blistering / skin detachment >10% BSA (Nikolsky sign positive)** and **rapid progression** (hours). Do not treat as EM – escalate immediately.
- **Airway compromise in severe mucosal EM:** Look for **stridor, hoarseness, or drooling** – indicates laryngeal oedema; call anaesthetics / ICU. Rare but life-threatening if missed.
- **Sepsis from secondary infection:** If blisters/erosions become infected, **systemic inflammatory response (tachycardia, hypotension, raised lactate)** – treat as per local sepsis protocol.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Acute onset (days) of symmetrical rash, often on **extremities** (hands, feet, extensor surfaces) ± trunk
  - **Target lesions** – three zones: central dusky/purple, middle pale/vesicular, outer erythematous rim
  - **Mucosal involvement** in EM major (oral, genital, ocular) – painful erosions, haemorrhagic crusts
  - Prodrome: mild fever, malaise (especially if mycoplasma or HSV trigger)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - **Targets** – typical (concentric rings) or **atypical** (only two zones, more raised)
  - **Acantholysis** (positive Nikolsky) is **absent** in EM – if present, suspect SJS/TEN
  - **Mucosal erosions** – check lips, buccal mucosa, conjunctivae, genitalia
  - No widespread blistering or sheet-like detachment

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Urticaria** – wheals are evanescent, not fixed target lesions; no mucosal involvement
  - **Drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms (DRESS)** – fever, lymphadenopathy, eosinophilia, internal organ involvement; EM lacks eosinophilia
  - **Fixed drug eruption** – single or few round, dark plaques; recur at same site after drug re-exposure
  - **Bullous pemphigoid** – tense blisters on flexural areas; elderly; not target lesions
  - **Vasculitis (e.g. IgA vasculitis)** – palpable purpura, often on lower legs; not target lesions
  - **Pityriasis rosea** – "herald patch" followed by Christmas tree pattern; no mucosal involvement
  - **SJS/TEN** – rapid blistering, Nikolsky positive, >10% BSA detachment, severe constitutional symptoms
- **Splitter:** **Nikolsky sign** – positive in SJS/TEN, negative in EM major with mucosal involvement. Also: **skin detachment >10% BSA** is not seen in EM.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - **Nikolsky sign** – gentle lateral pressure on erythematous skin; if blister forms → SJS/TEN
  - Assess **% BSA of skin detachment** (using Wallace rule of nines)
  - Check **mucosal surfaces** (oral, ocular, genital) with good light
- **Bloods:**
  - **FBC, U&Es, LFTs, CRP** – to rule out infection, organ involvement
  - **Serology for Mycoplasma pneumoniae** (IgM + cold agglutinins) – if respiratory prodrome
  - **HSV serology / PCR** – if recurrent or genital lesions
  - **If drug-induced** – eosinophil count may be raised (but more suggestive DRESS)
- **Imaging:**
  - **Chest X-ray** – if mycoplasma suspected (interstitial pneumonitis)
- **Special tests:**
  - **Skin biopsy for histology** (if diagnostic uncertainty) – full-thickness epidermal necrosis suggests SJS/TEN; EM shows interface dermatitis with apoptotic keratinocytes
  - **Direct immunofluorescence** – not routine; may help exclude autoimmune blistering disorders
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **Bastuji-Garin criteria** – distinguishes EM from SJS/TEN: EM has **target lesions** and **no** widespread skin detachment; SJS/TEN have >30% BSA detachment (with or without targets)
  - **SCORTEN** (for SJS/TEN severity) – not validated for EM
- **Never-miss test:** **Skin biopsy for histology** if doubt between EM major and SJS/TEN – omission may lead to inappropriate treatment (e.g. steroids in SJS/TEN) or failure to escalate.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Stop any suspicious drug** – document drug history (all current and recent)
  - **Assess airway** if severe oral mucosal involvement; if stridor, call anaesthetics
  - **IV fluids** if unable to swallow / dehydrated (0.9% sodium chloride at maintenance)
  - **Analgesia** – oral paracetamol (1g QDS); for severe mucosal pain, consider IV morphine (2.5-5mg PRN) – [check local policy]
  - **If moderate/severe mucosal involvement** – admit to dermatology or medical ward
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - **Supportive care** – wound care (non-adherent dressings, chlorhexidine baths), mouth care (benzydamine spray, oral antifungal if needed)
  - **Treat underlying cause**:
    - **HSV-associated** – oral aciclovir 200mg 5x/day for 5-7 days (if herpes lesions present) – but EM often begins after HSV, so antivirals may not alter current episode. For recurrent EM, consider prophylactic aciclovir 400mg BD for 6-12 months (NICE guidance)
    - **Mycoplasma-associated** – treat with clarithromycin 500mg BD PO for 7 days (or azithromycin)
    - **Drug-induced** – stop offending drug; avoid re-exposure
  - **Systemic corticosteroids** – controversial; may be considered in severe EM major with significant mucosal involvement (e.g. prednisolone 0.5-1 mg/kg/day PO for 5-7 days) – but **do not give if SJS/TEN cannot be excluded** – [check local policy]
  - **Eye care** – if ocular involvement, urgent ophthalmology referral; topical lubricants and steroid drops if corneal ulceration
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Recurrent EM** – identify triggers (HSV, drugs); consider prophylactic aciclovir (as above)
  - **Patient education** – avoid NSAIDs, anticonvulsants, sulphonamides if history of drug-induced EM
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride at 1-2 ml/kg/hr if nil by mouth; otherwise encourage oral hydration
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** risk assess – if immobile, use enoxaparin 40mg SC daily (adjust for weight/renal function per BNF)
  - **Diet:** soft/liquid diet if oral erosions; NBM if airway risk
  - **Observations:** baseline NEWS2, then 4-hourly; escalate if NEWS ≥5 or deterioration

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Analgesia:** Paracetamol 1g PO QDS (max 4g/day). If severe: morphine sulfate 2.5-5mg IV PRN (with naloxone available)
  - **Mouth care:** Benzydamine 0.15% spray or 0.3% oral rinse 5-10ml up to 6 times/day
  - **If HSV trigger (prophylaxis for recurrent EM):** Aciclovir 400mg PO BD for 6-12 months (renally adjust – CrCl <25 ml/min: 200mg BD)
  - **If Mycoplasma trigger:** Clarithromycin 500mg PO BD for 7 days (or azithromycin 500mg PO OD for 3 days)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Corticosteroids (if severe EM major, no SJS/TEN):** Prednisolone 40-60mg PO OD for 5-7 days then taper
  - **Antihistamines** (for pruritus): Cetirizine 10mg PO OD
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Aciclovir: caution in renal impairment (reduce dose)
  - Clarithromycin: QT prolongation risk – check ECG; interacts with statins, warfarin, digoxin
  - Prednisolone: avoid in active infection; consider varicella zoster risk (check immunity)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Aciclovir: CrCl 10-25 ml/min – 200mg BD (prophylaxis); CrCl <10 – 200mg OD
  - Clarithromycin: severe hepatic impairment – reduce dose or avoid
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Prednisolone in SJS/TEN** – giving systemic steroids in early SJS/TEN may worsen outcome (increased infection/mortality). **Never start steroids until EM confirmed – if doubt, hold and discuss with dermatology.**

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - **Secondary bacterial infection** of erosions (Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus pyogenes) – treat with flucloxacillin 500mg QDS PO or IV depending on severity
  - **Ocular complications** – conjunctivitis, corneal ulceration (rare in EM minor; more in major)
  - **Dehydration from poor oral intake** – especially in children
  - **Severe oral scarring** (rare)
- **Chronic:**
  - **Recurrent EM** – can occur every few weeks/months (HSV-driven)
  - **Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation** – resolves over months; no scarring if no infection
  - **Chronic ocular sequelae** – dry eye, symblepharon (rare) – need ophthalmology follow-up

### Prognosis
- **EM minor** – self-limiting in 2-4 weeks; full recovery, <5% recurrence.
- **EM major** – lasts 3-6 weeks with mucosal involvement; mortality <1% (if no progression to SJS/TEN). Recurrence rate higher if HSV trigger (up to 20%).
- **Prognosis** is excellent with supportive care; most patients do not require hospital admission.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always take a thorough drug history** including over-the-counter, herbal, and recent vaccinations – medication triggers are common.
- **Check for recurrent HSV** – ask about cold sores in the 2-4 weeks before rash. If recurrent EM, prescribe prophylactic aciclovir (even if no active HSV) after discussion with dermatology.
- **Document target lesions photographically** – helps monitor progression and differentiate from SJS/TEN. Use bedside ruler for scale.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not give systemic steroids empirically** – if mucosal involvement is severe but you cannot exclude SJS/TEN, steroids are contraindicated. Escalate to dermatology first.
- **Do not discharge without eye assessment** – even subtle conjunctival injection may indicate early ocular involvement; if missed, can lead to chronic dry eye.
- **Do not confuse atypical target lesions with urticaria** – urticarial wheals last <24 hours at a site; EM lesions are fixed for days. Antihistamines alone will not resolve EM.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - **Rash worsens or blisters enlarge** – new blistering beyond original areas or Nikolsky positive
  - **Difficulty swallowing, breathing, or stridor** – indicates laryngeal oedema (call 999)
  - **Signs of infection** – fever, oozing/redness around eroded areas, spreading erythema
  - **Eye pain, photophobia, or red eye** – indicates corneal involvement (urgent ophthalmology)
- **Follow-up:** GP review in 2 weeks; if recurrent, refer to dermatology. If ocular involvement, ophthalmology follow-up in 1 week.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / dermatology if:**
  - Uncertainty between EM major and SJS/TEN (worst-case scenario)
  - Severe mucosal involvement (oral/ocular/genital) – requiring IV fluids, ophthalmology
  - Rapid progression of blisters/new target lesions over >30% BSA in <48 hours
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**
  - **NEWS2 ≥7** or any single parameter meets criteria (respiratory rate >30, hypotension SBP <90, GCS <13)
  - **Airway compromise** (stridor, drooling, hoarseness)
  - **Lactate >4 mmol/L** or **oliguria <0.5 ml/kg/hr** for >2 hours (signs of sepsis from skin infection)
